text.title: MVP Scoping Anti-Patterns
text.summary: Seven failure modes that produce useless or over-built MVPs, with root causes and fixes.

antipattern.description: Must-Have list contains more than 5 features or exceeds 60% of capacity.
antipattern.reason: Every additional Must is scope creep disguised as necessity. Cap is 3–5 items; above that, re-examine whether each directly tests the hypothesis.

antipattern.description: MVP has no kill criterion — only a success target.
antipattern.reason: Without a kill threshold the team always finds a reason to keep going; the MVP becomes a permanent v1. Force the field: "If metric < X by date Y, pivot or kill."
Six months is not an MVP — it is a product launch. Real MVPs fit inside 2–8 weeks. If the timebox is longer, the scope is wrong or the hypothesis is too broad.

antipattern.description: Won't-Have is empty or has fewer than 3 items.
antipattern.reason: The Won't-Have list is the scope-creep firewall. An empty list means no trade-offs were made. Every MVP should explicitly exclude ≥ 3 tempting features with stated reasons.

antipattern.description: MVP has no learning goal — only a feature list.
antipattern.reason: Features without questions are just engineering work. The learning goal is what converts the MVP from "release" to "experiment"; if it is absent, there is nothing to learn from the launch.

antipattern.description: Authentication and billing listed as Must-Have for v0.
antipattern.reason: Magic links replace full auth. Stripe checkout replaces in-app billing UI. Both are Should for v0 unless the hypothesis is specifically about the billing or auth experience.

antipattern.description: The same agent that proposes features also reviews scope.
antipattern.reason: Proposer bias inflates the Must list. Use a separate reviewer subagent that applies the 5-question quick check independently.
